object.plain_definition: The range is the difference between the highest and lowest values in a dataset, providing a simple measure of the data's total spread.

school_usage.HGSE: Typically used in summarizing student assessment scores to identify the gap between top and bottom performing cohorts.
school_usage.HBS: Commonly employed in case study analytics to highlight the breadth of variation in market performance metrics.
school_usage.FAS: Frequently utilized in administrative reports to characterize the diversity in grant funding amounts across various faculty projects.
school_usage.HMS: Often applied to clinical trial baseline characteristics to describe the minimum and maximum age or physiological metrics of participants.
school_usage.SEAS: Typically used when analyzing sensor data or experimental throughput to define the operational limits of a specific apparatus.

object.body_markdown: ## Plain-language definition The range is the difference between the highest and lowest values in a dataset, providing a simple measure of the data's total spread. ## What it tells you It defines the extreme boundaries of your observations. By showing the full span of values, it reveals the total distance between the least and greatest points, offering a quick sense of scope without considering the internal distribution. ## When it matters You reach for the range when you need to understand the absolute limits of a phenomenon: - Monitoring the diversity of responses in a survey - Identifying the span of time required to process internal requests - Establishing minimums and maximums for quality control thresholds - Assessing the variety in resource allocation across different departments ## Common confusion with standard deviation The range is highly sensitive to outliers; one extremely high value significantly inflates the range. Unlike standard deviation, which measures how most data clusters around the center, the range only considers the two most extreme points. If your dataset contains a single typo or an erroneous entry, the range will be misleading, while the standard deviation will remain more stable. ## Example in context A central office tracks the time it takes to onboard new staff. They note that the process takes between 2 and 45 days. The range is 43 days. While this shows the full span, it doesn't reveal if most people wait 5 days or 40 days. The range highlights the extreme backlog in one department, prompting a review of that specific process rather than the general onboarding program. ## Usage across Harvard At HGSE and HBS, outcome reports for graduating cohorts typically lead with the median salary because graduate outcomes often skew right. In clinical research at HMS, median survival time is the standard summary for time-to-event data because the mean can be undefined when patients are still alive at study end. Institutional research reports on giving or compensation at the central level generally include both, though the median is the more informative number in both cases. ## Related terms - Variance: The average squared deviation from the mean, showing how spread out numbers are from the center. - Standard Deviation: The square root of the variance, providing a measure of spread in the same units as the data. - Interquartile Range: The range of the middle 50 percent of data, which is much more robust against outliers than the full range. - Minimum: The smallest value in the set. - Maximum: The largest value in the set.
